title: SSH invalid or failed login observed
why_it_matters: Failed SSH authentication can indicate password spraying, brute force, or attempted initial access.
false_positive_notes: Internet-facing systems often receive background noise; correlate with source IP, frequency, and successful logins.
recommended_next_steps: Review source IPs, usernames, timing, accepted sessions, and firewall/VPN context.

title: SSH accepted login observed
why_it_matters: Accepted SSH logins identify potential access windows and accounts used during compromise.
false_positive_notes: Accepted logins may be normal administration; validate user, source, and timing.
recommended_next_steps: Correlate with authorized key provenance, command history, sudo logs, and network source.

title: SSH authorized key present
why_it_matters: Authorized keys can be used for persistent access without passwords.
false_positive_notes: Validate keys against approved admin inventory.
recommended_next_steps: Review key ownership, comments, mtime, provenance, and associated authentication logs.

title: Root-equivalent account observed
why_it_matters: UID 0 accounts have root-equivalent privilege and may indicate account tampering.
false_positive_notes: The normal root account is expected; focus on additional UID 0 records.
recommended_next_steps: Compare against baseline and inspect account modification logs.

title: Suspicious shell history command
why_it_matters: Shell history containing download, execution, or network utility commands can identify attacker activity.
false_positive_notes: Admins use these commands legitimately; validate context and timing.
recommended_next_steps: Correlate command history with process, network, package, and file modification evidence.

title: SSH key provenance artifact
why_it_matters: SSH key provenance helps distinguish approved access from suspicious persistence.
false_positive_notes: Expected keys should map to known admins, automation, or configuration management.
recommended_next_steps: Validate hashes, file mtimes, owners, comments, and recent account access.

title: Suspicious PAM authentication change
why_it_matters: PAM changes can enable backdoors, credential theft, or authentication bypass.
false_positive_notes: Enterprise identity tooling may add PAM modules; validate against approved configuration.
recommended_next_steps: Preserve PAM configs and compare against package defaults and known-good baselines.

title: UID/GID account anomaly
why_it_matters: Unexpected UID/GID or shell anomalies can indicate privilege persistence or account tampering.
false_positive_notes: Some appliances have nonstandard accounts; validate against asset baseline.
recommended_next_steps: Review passwd/group/shadow evidence and recent account management logs.

title: LD preload rootkit indicator
why_it_matters: LD preload abuse can hook process behavior and hide malicious activity.
false_positive_notes: Approved EDR or profiling tooling may use preload behavior; validate file path and owner.
recommended_next_steps: Preserve preload file and referenced libraries before remediation.

title: systemd service or timer persistence
why_it_matters: systemd units and timers are common persistence mechanisms.
false_positive_notes: Most Linux systems have many legitimate units; validate path, owner, ExecStart, and recent changes.
recommended_next_steps: Inspect unit files, drop-ins, timers, writable paths, and referenced binaries.

title: Linux file capability observed
why_it_matters: Unexpected file capabilities may grant privileged behavior without setuid.
false_positive_notes: Some packaged binaries legitimately use capabilities; compare against package baseline.
recommended_next_steps: Review package ownership, hash, mtime, path, and whether the capability is expected.

title: Deleted running file observed
why_it_matters: Deleted-but-running files can indicate evasive malware or replaced executables still active in memory.
false_positive_notes: Package upgrades can temporarily produce deleted open files; validate process context.
recommended_next_steps: Preserve process, command line, open file handles, and memory-relevant context before killing it.
